caption: Management's 2016 investment case and five-part barrier framework: scale, insurer relationships, permitted land, technology, and operating complexity.
The 2016 risk frame quantified foreign buyers at roughly 20% of U.S. units and linked ADAS, scrap, and currency to supply and auction proceeds.
The salvage equation shows how pre-accident value, repair and rental cost, and salvage value determine an insurer total-loss decision.
The original demand tree decomposes salvage supply into miles driven, crashes per mile, and salvage rate, with explicit positive and negative drivers.
The 20/20/20 yard program targeted 20 expansions and 20 new yards in 20 months with about $100 million of 2016 capital spending.
The U.K. case study traces acquisition, conversion from principal to agency, insurer outsourcing, yard expansion, and the first two million vehicle sales.
New-member registrations rose from roughly 260,000 in 2012 to more than 420,000 in 2015, illustrating buyer-network expansion.
The 2016 yard map shows more than 150 permitted U.S. sites and the regional density required by insurers, including a 20-year Southern California land pursuit.
Process-complexity evidence includes 200,000-plus weekly bids, a 4,000-provider subhaul network, multi-jurisdiction title work, and catastrophe surge spending.
The technology timeline dates CAS, online images, VB1, online-only VB2, international deployments, public access, and mobile bidding.
Copart's 2016 ADAS frame cited an estimated 10-15% maximum accident reduction at full penetration; useful as a dated expectation, not a current forecast.
The installed-fleet replacement chart shows why even high new-vehicle ADAS adoption would diffuse slowly through a 256 million vehicle fleet.
North American same-store revenue grew in nine of ten years through FY2015, averaging 6.7%, with volume, used values, scrap, FX, and fees as drivers.
The historical EBITDA-versus-land/non-land-capex chart shows the physical reinvestment embedded in Copart's cash-generation model.
The FY2006-1H2016 repurchase history shows highly episodic capital returns, including a $735 million FY2011 peak.
Management's 2016 structural-protection framework links consignment, netted receivables, conservative capitalization, and natural frequency/severity offsets.
